coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Ce, N co–doped carbon dots as green and effective corrosion inhibitor for copper in acid environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Shenyou
Zhang, Fan
Liu, Yuehua
Ouyang, Shuilin
Ye, Y.W.
Chen, H.
coredata.dc:description: Background: A new high-effectively corrosion inhibitor was used to replace the traditional corrosion inhibitor and reduce the environment pollution. Methods: Through hydrothermal synthesis, Ce and N co-doped carbon dots (Ce@N-CDs) were generated. Before the performance characterization, the microstructure and chemical composition of Ce@N-CDs were firstly investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. The electrochemical test revealed that the IE of Ce-10% (200 mg/L) on copper was as high as 98%, presenting the best inhibition effect. According to the adsorption isotherm analysis, the Ce@N-CDs mainly adsorbed according to the Langmuir model, and the adsorption process was completed by combining physical and chemical adsorption. Significant findings: Through the morphology, energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and XPS analysis of copper surface after corrosion process, it was found that the Ce@N-CDs formed a dense adsorption/passivation film on the copper surface. The adsorption film mainly came from the pairing of unsaturated bonds between N-doped CDs (N-CDs) and copper surface. The passivation film mainly came from the oxide formed by the reaction between Ce and the surrounding environment. Their dense structure could effectively inhibit the infiltration and erosion of corrosive medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ce
N-CDs
Copper
Tafel
Corrosion inhibitor
Characterization analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An illustration of the corrosion inhibitor preparation process.
Structural information of Ce@N-CDs corrosion inhibitors (a) FTIR, (b) XPS, (c) XPS-Ce2p, (d) XPS-N1s, (e) XPS-C1s.
EIS results of copper after 10 h immersion in HCl solution containing various Ce@N-CDs inhibitors (a)(b) N-CDs, (c)(d) Ce-5%, (e)(f) Ce-10%, (g)(h) Ce-15%.
The equivalent circuit of corrosion process.
Polarization curves of copper immersion in the test solution. (a) N-CDs, (b) Ce-5%, (c) Ce-10%, (d) Ce-15%.
Three-dimensional morphology and surface roughness of copper after immersion.
Two-dimensional morphology and EDS analysis of copper surface after corrosion.
Corrosion inhibition rate of copper in each test solution.
XPS spectral results of the corroded sample surface.
Isotherm adsorption curves of (a) N-CDs, (b) Ce-5%, (c) Ce-10%, (d) Ce-15%.
EIS value of copper in test solution containing corrosion inhibitor.
Potentiodynamic polarization parameters of copper in the test solution.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Ce, N co–doped carbon dots as green and effective corrosion inhibitor for copper in acid environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFaculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Rear Earth, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Rear Earth, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Background: A new high-effectively corrosion inhibitor was used to replace the traditional corrosion inhibitor and reduce the environment pollution.

Methods: Through hydrothermal synthesis, Ce and N co-doped carbon dots (Ce@N-CDs) were generated. Before the performance characterization, the microstructure and chemical composition of Ce@N-CDs were firstly investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. The electrochemical test revealed that the IE of Ce-10% (200 mg/L) on copper was as high as 98%, presenting the best inhibition effect. According to the adsorption isotherm analysis, the Ce@N-CDs mainly adsorbed according to the Langmuir model, and the adsorption process was completed by combining physical and chemical adsorption.

Significant findings: Through the morphology, energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and XPS analysis of copper surface after corrosion process, it was found that the Ce@N-CDs formed a dense adsorption/passivation film on the copper surface. The adsorption film mainly came from the pairing of unsaturated bonds between N-doped CDs (N-CDs) and copper surface. The passivation film mainly came from the oxide formed by the reaction between Ce and the surrounding environment. Their dense structure could effectively inhibit the infiltration and erosion of corrosive medium.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to its stable physical and chemical properties and excellent thermal conductivity, copper is widely used in many important industrial fields, such as construction, electrical, shipbuilding, petrochemical, etc. [1]. However, in some acidic media, especially in the presence of aggressive ions such as Cl−, the corrosion of copper will be more serious, which will bring great threats to human production and life [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop effective strategies to eliminate/mitigate copper corrosion. Tan et al. [3] pointed out that one of the ways to eliminate/mitigate undesirable corrosion is to use corrosion inhibitors, because they can adsorb on the metal surface to form a protective film and reduce the contact area between the corrosion solution and metal [4]. In recent years, the role of corrosion inhibitor in controlling/eliminating metal corrosion has been deeply studied [5], and the inhibition effect of many inorganic and organic compounds has been reported [6]. However, many of these corrosion inhibitors are highly toxic, which is not conducive to the sustainable development of green environmental protection. This phenomenon forces scientists to search for corrosion inhibitors with environmental protection, low cost and convenient preparation.

Carbon dots are multifunctional nanomaterials with many advantages [7], including enormous biocompatibility, high quantum yield, strong adsorption capacity, low cost, eco-friendliness, high solubility, and excellent stability [8]. Due to these outstanding properties, carbon dots are suitable for photocatalysis, biosensing, drug delivery, bioimaging and other biological fields [9]. At present, carbon dots are prepared by many researchers through hydrothermal method [10], laser burning method [11], and microwave irradiation method [12]. On this basis, the structure and properties of carbon dots can be effectively optimized by doping [13]. Many researchers use carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors, and find that they have good corrosion inhibition effects on different metals in different environments. In order to obtain a single precursor, He et al. [14] used common sugars (glucose, soluble starch, fructose or sucrose) as raw materials. The anticorrosion efficiency of in-situ synthesized carbon dots on steel reached approximately 95% at a light precursor concentration of 0.26%. Liu et al. [15] decomposed natural citric acid derivatives at high temperature (150, 180, 210 °C) to obtain three N-CDs corrosion inhibitors, and explored their corrosion inhibition performances for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution. It was found that citric acid-based carbon dots could generate a dense adsorption film on interface. Wan et al. [16] used amino-functionalized carbon dots as intercalating agents to exfoliate and modify boron nitride to obtain carbon dot modified boron nitride nanocomposites. After modification, boron nitride was dispersed in water-based epoxy resin matrix, and the corrosion rate of copper substrate was greatly reduced by the modified coatings. At the same time, the rare earth element of Ce can form a dense passivation film on the metal surface under certain conditions, which can greatly improve the protection of metal. Gobara et al. [17] studied the corrosion behavior of Ce-doped melamine on aluminum alloys in 3.5% NaCl solution, and confirmed that the doping of Ce ions could help enhance the protective properties of corrosion inhibitor. Verma et al. [18] investigated the structure of Ce-passive layer on the metal surface, and pointed out that the Ce3+ could be converted into Ce(OH)3 and Ce2O3 to inhibit the metal corrosion. Mishra et al. [19] researched the corrosion behavior of BTESE and Ce(NO3)3 on zinc specimen, and illustrated that the self-healing corrosion effect of cerium nitrate on zinc was found by polarization study.

In this work, Ce@N-CDs was prepared from cerium nitrate and ammonium citrate, and its corrosion inhibition effect on copper in 1 M HCl solution was tested to further enhance the corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs. Meantime, the corrosion mechanism of copper in corrosive solution with as-prepared Ce@N-CDs inhibitor was revealed by analyzing the influence of Ce content on the corrosion inhibition behavior of Ce@N-CDs.

In this research work, copper (T2) was used as electrode material in all experiments. As the test solution, it was composed of distilled water, 36%∼38% HCl solution and various concentrations of corrosion inhibitor (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L). Cerium nitrate and ammonium citrate were two basic raw materials for preparing corrosion inhibitors, which was provided by Jiangsu Convoy Science and Education Equipment Co., Ltd.

The corrosion inhibitor was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis, and the specific preparation process was shown in Fig. 1 . Different masses (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 g) of cerium nitrate and 2 g of ammonium citrate were dissolved in 60 ml of deionized water, and then the mixed solution was stirred for 10 mins and ultrasonically treated for 15 mins. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to the reaction kettle and placed in an oven at 473 K for 8 h. When the reaction was completed, the product was taken out after natural cooling and then conducted three times of vacuum filtration. After that, the filtrate was dialyzed through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000, and the distilled water was changed every 6 h. Finally, the obtained solution was dried in an oven at a temperature of 353 K. Based on the mass ratio of cerium nitrate and ammonium citrate, the four corrosion inhibitors were abbreviated as N-CDs, Ce-5%, Ce-10%, and Ce-15% for expression convenience.

The NICOLET 6700 FTIR was applied to measure the structural characteristics of Ce@N-CDs. The Axis Ultra DLD XPS was employed to analyze the elemental composition and content of Ce@N-CDs. By using the JEM2100 TEM, the morphology and dispersion of Ce@N-CDs were discovered.

The mass loss measurement method was utilized to analyze the corrosion rate of copper. To begin, 400, 800, and 1200 grit sandpapers were successively used to polish the sample, which was 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm in size. Then the copper samples were immersed in ethanol for ultrasonic cleaning to remove the impurities on the sample surface. After that, the sample was dried, weighed before the immersion test, and then immersed in 1 M HCl solution containing different Ce@N-CDs concentrations. Finally, the samples were cleaned with deionized water, dried, and placed in an analytical balance to be weighed.

Electrochemical tests were performed on a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation. All electrochemical tests were conducted using a three-electrode test system [20], which included a working electrode (copper), a counter electrode (platinum electrode), and a reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode). The working electrode was made of a sample covered with AB glue, leaving only 1 cm × 1 cm surface exposed to the test solution. Before electrochemical tests, a stable open circuit potential (EOCP) was obtained. The amplitude of sinusoidal potential was 20 mV, and the measurement frequency of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was 100 kHz∼0.01 Hz. The polarization curves were carried out with EOCP ± 0.3 V, and the scanning rate was 0.001 V/s 21,22.

The samples were immersed in 1 M HCl with varying concentrations of corrosion inhibitors. After immersion, they were washed with distilled water and then dried for analysis. The 2D morphology, 3D topography and surface roughness of samples were measured by scanning electron microscope and scanning confocal microscope. The chemical components of corrosion products on the sample surface were analyzed by XPS, and EDS was used to describe the elemental distribution of corrosion products.

XPS was employed to examine the elemental compositions of the as-prepared Ce@N-CDs, and the results were shown in Table 1 . The table revealed that the major elements of the as-prepared inhibitor were C, O, Ce and N. Ce atoms were successfully doped [23]. It was proved that the content of cerium nitrate increased from 0% to 15%, while the content of N and O elements reduced from 9.1% and 31.3% to 7.5% and 27.9%, respectively.

Fig. 2 presented the bonding state and composition of Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. As illustrated in Fig. 2a, two broad absorption peaks were observed in the FTIR spectrum at 3402.3 and 2836.2 cm−1, which corresponded to N-H and C-H stretching vibrations. The sp [3] hybridization carbon atoms (C-N and C-O) were considered to be the cause of the peaks at 1296.1 and 1436.9 cm−1. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1538.1, 1647.3 and 1693.9 cm−1 were attributed to sp2-hybridized carbon atoms (C = N, -CO-NH- and C = O). In addition, the peak at 2309.3 cm−1 was caused by -CC- stretching vibration. Fig. 2b showed the XPS full spectrum analysis of Ce@N-CDs. Four peaks of Ce, N, C and O could be seen in the full spectrum. The fine spectra of Ce, N, and C were acquired by Gaussian fitting [24] to analyze the existential forms. Four peaks at 885.0, 904.1 eV and 888.7, 907.3 eV could be respectively ascribed to Ce3+ and Ce4+ in Ce2p spectrum [25] (Fig. 2c). The N1s spectrum could be fitted with an N-H peak at 399.5 eV [26] and an N-C peak at 402 eV [27] (Fig. 2d). The peaks at 284.4, 285.4, 287.4, and 290.5 eV were discovered in C1s spectrum [26], corresponding to C-N, C-C, O = C-N, C = O, respectively (Fig. 2e). These results were corroborated with the FTIR spectrum.

The high-resolution morphology and distribution of Ce@N-CDs were shown in Fig. 3 . According to the TEM images, the corrosion inhibitor was spherical or quasi-spherical particles with a relatively uniform distribution. At the same time, it was found that the size was around 2∼5 nm, which was a typical nanocrystalline particle [28]. Moreover, through magnification analysis, it was found that there were obvious lattice fringes in the morphology of the as-prepared Ce@N-CDs, and the interplanar spacing was about 0.22 nm, presenting a high crystalline structure.

Corrosion inhibitors with and without Ce were added into 1 M HCl solution, and their corrosion inhibition effect on copper was investigated. The result of Nyquist and Bode images was displayed in Fig. 4 . After 10 h immersion, at the same inhibitor concentration, it could be seen that N-CDs (without Ce element) presented the smallest capacitive arc radius in the Nyquist diagram and the low-frequency (0.01 Hz) impedance modulus in the Bode diagram. The larger capacitive arc radius and low-frequency impedance modulus suggested that the doping of Ce improved the protective performance of N-CDs. For the inhibitor solution containing different proportions of Ce element, the Ce-10% sample exhibited the largest capacitive arc radius and the highest low-frequency impedance modulus at the same inhibitor concentration. In the meantime, as the inhibitor concentration increased, the low-frequency impedance modulus exhibited an uptrend as a whole, meaning that the presence of inhibitor would build a protective barrier layer [29]. In addition, the capacitive arc radius and low-frequency impedance modulus of Ce@N-CDs first increased and then decreased with the content of Ce element. This was because Ce@N-CDs could form an efficient obstacle film at the copper/solution surface, which augmented the charge transfer resistance and effectively inhibited copper corrosion as the increase of Ce within a certain content range [21]. However, when the Ce content was too high, the Ce@N-CDs was not completely attached to the surface of metal due to the cluster effect [30], resulting in a decrease in the corrosion inhibition ability.

According to the EIS information obtained by the CHI-660E electrochemical workstation, the equivalent circuit was shown in Fig. 5 after fitting. The solution resistance, polarization resistance, constant phase element of the electric double layer were represented by parameter R s, R p (=R f+R ct), and C dl, respectively. Table 2 displayed the fitting results of these parameters. It was obvious that the R p value was a crucial index to reflect the resistance of adsorbed film to corrosive ions and the resistance of metal surface to electron transmission [31]. The increase in R p value could be a result of the change in adsorption film thickness and coverage area. The exposure area of electrode and the dielectric constant of adsorption film decreased with the increase of electric double layer thickness. The R p value in the Ce@N-CDs solution was larger than that in the N-CDs environment during the whole test period, indicating that the adsorption difficulty of inhibitor on the metal surface was reduced after the doping of Ce. The R p value also gradually increased as Ce@N-CDs concentration increased, implying that an effective adsorption film covered on the copper surface. Additionally, the C dl value showed a different trend to R p value. This result was due to the gradual replacement of corrosive medium on the copper surface by Ce@N-CDs. The Helmholtz model [32] was used to calculate C dl. (Eq. (1)):
(1)
C
d l
=
ε
0
ε
d
S
where ε 0 and ε were the dielectric constants of the atmosphere and the thin films, respectively. The thickness of electric double layer was denoted by d. S stood for the exposure ratio of electrode. As Ce@N-CDs concentration increased, the adsorption of Ce@N-CDs on the copper/solution interface led to an augment in the thickness of the electric double layer capacitor [22]. Thus, the C dl value displayed a downward trend as Ce@N-CDs concentration increased, indicating that more corrosion ions were replaced by Ce@N-CDs and the corrosion inhibition capacity was improved after adding Ce@N-CDs inhibitor.

In a specific corrosive environments, potentiodynamic polarization was a widely used electrochemical method to evaluate the corrosion rate and inhibition effect of corrosion inhibitor on metals [33]. Fig. 6 depicted the Tafel curves of copper in corrosion medium. Obviously, these curves consisted of cathode and anode parts. The characteristics of Tafel curve did not clearly change with or without Ce@N-CDs, demonstrating that the corrosion mechanism of copper remained unchanged [34]. In general, the higher the corrosion potential was, the lower the corrosion tendency of electrode was. By comparison, it could be seen that the Tafel curve of electrode moved to the right as a whole, which may be due to the formation of a protective film on the metal surface. Moreover, compared with the blank sample, the anode part of the Tafel curve became steeper after adding Ce@N-CDs, which indicated that the Ce@N-CDs mainly inhibited the corrosion of the anode.

By analyzing the polarization curve concretely, Table 3 gave the relevant corrosion parameters, which contained corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), surface coverage (θ) and inhibition efficiency (IE) from left to right. The E corr in blank solution was about −525 mV. vs. SCE, which was lower than that in other environments. Because of the inhibitor adsorption on the surface, the i corr of copper showed a downward trend as inhibitor concentration increased. Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) were used to compute the surface coverage [35] (θ) and the inhibition efficiency [36] (IE).
(2)
θ =
I
c o r r
0
−
I
c o r r
I
c o r r
0
(3)
I E =
I
c o r r
0
−
I
c o r r
I
c o r r
0
× 100 %

From Table 3, it could be concluded that IE and i corr revealed an opposite trend. Under the same inhibitor concentration, the IE of N-CDs, Ce-5%, Ce-10%, Ce-15% samples first increased and then decreased, and reached the maximum when the Ce content was 10%. In terms of concentration, in Ce-10% solution, the IE values were 88%, 97%, 97%, and 98% as inhibitor concentration increased. At Ce-10% concentration of 200 mg/L, the IE reached the maximum value (98%), and the R p also reached the maximum value (2506.1 Ω·cm [2]). Therefore, when the addition content of Ce was 10%, the inhibition effect was the best, and at this time, it could effectively shield the corrosion of aggressive ions [37]. Compared with previous studies, the IE of Ce@N-CDs was higher than that of N-doped carbon dots (88.9%) [38], Rollinia occidentalis extract (85.1%) [39], Konjac glucomannan (92.4%) [40], Pyridazinium ionic liquids (84.0%) [41], Salvia officinalis plant extract (87.0%) [42], Ginkgo leaf extract (92.5%) [43] and Schinopsis lorentzii extract (86.0%) [44].

The three-dimensional morphology analysis of copper surface with and without Ce@N-CDs treatment was listed in Fig. 7 . The average roughness (Ra) of electrode surface after immersion in blank solution for 10 h was 0.445 μm. In contrast, after the electrodes were immersed in 200 mg/L of Ce@N-CDs solutions for 10 h, the Ra values were 0.274, 0.270, 0.201, 0.230 μm when the Ce content was 0%, 5%, 10%, and 15%, respectively. Among them, the Ce-10% sample exhibited the best corrosion inhibition effect, and the Ra of the electrode surface was reduced by 54.8% compared with blank sample in this case. In addition, by choosing Ce-10% sample as the research object, the decrease of inhibitor concentration and immersion time would lead to the weakening of the corrosion inhibition effect.

After the immersion test, the 2D morphology of copper and the distribution of elements on the copper surface were depicted in Fig. 8 . The electrode surface without corrosion inhibitor protection showed numerous protrusions and grooves because of severe corrosion. The existence of a protective inhibitor film greatly alleviated the corrosion of electrodes, and the difference of surface topography between electrodes with and without inhibitors was caused by the inherent changes from corrosion assault. With the increase of Ce content, the corrosion of the copper surface was first alleviated and then intensified. After the electrodes were immersed in 200 mg/L of Ce-10% environment for 10 h, the corrosion signs were the slightest. Besides, some pitting traces were observed on the 2D morphology at the condition of Ce10%−200 mg/L-4 h, which may be due to the uneven formation of the protective film in such short immersion time. Nevertheless, the large amount of pitting corrosion was found on the surface of electrode at the immersion condition of Ce10%−50 mg/L-10 h, this may be caused by insufficient concentration of Ce-10% and incomplete formation of Ce-based passivation film.

The EDS scanning results of element distribution on the local surface were also shown in Fig. 8. The Cu, C, and N elements were evenly dispersed around the copper surface according to the image. The Cu sign was from the substrate metal, the Cl sign came from the test solution, and the Ce, C as well as N signs came from Ce@N-CDs inhibitor. Meanwhile, a large amount of N and Ce elements were distributed on the surface of electrode, which also confirmed from the side that a dense adsorption/passivation film was formed on the surface.

The corrosion rate obtained by calculation was shown in Fig. 9 . Through analysis, the value of copper in inhibitor environment was significantly lower than that of the blank sample. At 200 mg/L and 10 h, the corrosion rate of copper in Ce-10% solution was the smallest, which was about 0.175 g/y. At the same time, in this case, the impedance modulus of the electrode was the maximum. When the Ce content in corrosion inhibitor was fixed at 10%, the corrosion rate of copper was 0.271 g/y under the condition of 50 mg/L and 10 h, and the corrosion rate of copper was 0.328 g/y under the condition of 200 mg/L and 4 h. From this, it could be concluded that the corrosion rate of copper was the smallest in the Ce-10% solution with a concentration of 200 mg/L and an immersion time of 10 h, which further confirmed the best corrosion inhibition effect.

XPS was used to analyze the electrode surface after immersion in the Ce@N-CDs solution for 10 h, the results were shown in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10a, the Ce2p spectrum was consisted of four peaks around 898.8, 902.1, 917.1, and 920.6 eV, corresponding to Ce-containing oxides [45–47] and hydroxides [47]. As shown in Fig. 10b, the N1s spectrum could be fitted with two peaks, corresponding to N-Ce/N-Cu and N-C, respectively. These indicated that the copper surface was covered by corrosion inhibitor, which contained passivation and adsorption films, thereby delaying the corrosion process. The principal ingredients of the passivation film were Ce-containing oxides and hydroxides. The main components of the adsorption film were as-prepared carbon dots.

The interface adsorption state could be analyzed by adsorption isotherms, which was related to the surface coverage (θ) of electrode and the inhibitor concentration (C) of corrosion solution. Adsorption isotherms could be used to describe interfacial interactions, which included Langmuir, Temkin, and Frumkin. By comprehensive consideration, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm was used in this study, and the isotherm was obtained by formula [48] (4).
(4)
C θ
= C +
1
K
a d s
where C was the inhibitor concentration, θ was the coverage of inhibitor, and K ads was adsorption-desorption equilibrium constant. Compared to other isotherms, the Langmuir isotherm provided the best fit degree (R 2=0.999). K ads value depended on the adsorption capacity of sample, and a larger value corresponded to a stronger adsorption capacity [49]. It could be seen from Fig. 11 that the K ads value exhibited a trend of initial rose and then drop as the Ce content increased, reaching its maximum value (K ads=168.1 L/g) at Ce content of 10%. This result was consistent with the weight loss experiment. The standard Gibbs free energy of
Δ
G
a d s
0
was a key physical quantity to research the adsorption type of corrosion inhibitors. A
Δ
G
a d s
0
value greater than −20 kJ/mol indicated a physical adsorption process, while a
Δ
G
a d s
0
value less than −40 kJ/mol indicated a chemisorption process [50].
Δ
G
a d s
0
could be acquired by Eq. (5) [51].
(5)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T l n
(
1000
K
a d s
)
where R was 8.314 J/(mol·K) and T was the absolute temperature. Through calculation, the
Δ
G
a d s
0
values were −28.9, −29.8, −29.8 and −29.6 kJ/mol in N-CDs, Ce-5%, Ce-10%, Ce-15% solution. By comparison, it could be concluded that the Ce@N-CDs was located on the metal surface through the synergistic effect of chemical and physical adsorption.

Fig.12 showed the protection mechanism diagram of copper in the corrosion solution. The electrochemical analysis showed that Ce@N-CDs mainly inhibited the anodic reaction of corrosion process, thereby reducing the corrosion rate of copper. The corrosion morphology analysis and adsorption type analysis further indicated that the inhibitor formed a protective film on interface through physicochemical adsorption. The electrostatic interaction between the protonated nitrogen atoms and the residual charges on the copper surface was the main reason for the formation of physisorption [52]. The coordination bonds between N-CDs and the copper surface were believed to be responsible for the chemisorption. The unprotonated nitrogen atoms on the N-CDs surface would interact with the unoccupied empty electron orbitals on the copper surface (N-Fe bond). In addition, the appearance of Ce-containing oxides and hydroxides in XPS result confirmed that the Ce-containing passivation film was formed on the copper surface through chemical reaction. The further corrosion of copper was inhibited by the combined action of adsorption film and passivation film.

A novel green inhibitor of Ce@N-CDs was successfully prepared on the basis of N-CDs, and its structure and properties were systematically characterized to control the corrosion inhibition behavior of copper in 1 M HCl environment. The representative conclusions were as follow:
(1)
The Ce@N-CDs with size of 2∼5 nm exhibited a spherical or quasi-spherical shape, and the distribution was relatively uniform.
(2)
The addition of Ce exhibited a great influence on the protection of copper. At 200 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency of Ce-10% was the maximum of 98%.
(3)
The K ads and
Δ
G
ads
0
showed that the adsorption nature of as-developed Ce@N-CDs was controlled by physical and chemical processes. These processes ensured the effective adsorption of Ce@N-CDs on the metal surface, thereby isolating them from corrosive media.
(4)
By analyzing the chemical composition of protective film, it was found that the main components were N-CDs adsorption film, Ce-containing oxide and hydroxide passivation film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52101086), Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (JXUSTQJYX2020010); Jiangxi Province ‘‘Double Thousand Plan” Innovation Leading Talent Short-term Project (jxsq2020007); Bureau of Science and Technology of Ganzhou Municipality; Distinguished Professor Program of Jinggang Scholars in institutions of higher learning, Jiangxi Province; Jiangxi Province ‘‘Double Thousand Plan” Science and Technology Innovation high-end Talent Project (jxsq2019201039).
